sentences.text: You couldn't--
I'm rolling.
And now's a good time.
I have here with me my younger daughter, Silvia Baroca.
Her married name is Baroca.
As she is, now, here in my house.
And she's very precious to me.
She's named after my mother.
And for some strange reason-- maybe somebody up there designed it that way-- she always reminds me of my mother.
She has the same mannerisms, and she is as good a person as my mother was.
And I do hope that she never goes or experiences what I experienced.
OK.
As soon as Mike tells you.
And great.
Let's continue, please.
We are now May 8, 1945.
Everybody is rejoicing.
We are actually free.
And then this feeling is frightening, because none of us-- we always hoped for the freedom.
We always, most of us, were waiting for the moment when the Nazis will be defeated, when the war will be over, when we can do what we want.
I mean, we're talking about who was going to feed us.
The food that we got was very, very minimal, but at least it was a program.
It was like you knew that you're going to get that ration, that you're going to have something.
Now, where is the-- there was no question of going shopping or having money.
That, that doesn't enter your mind.
But the fact of freedom-- you can go to the bathroom, you can do what you want, you're not restricted.
Because during those years, which I didn't mention-- I don't know if any of the survivors mentioned-- we were, had to report to go to the bathroom.
And they were watching you.
They were timing you.
They were mean.
They just didn't-- they said, you go too often, or you take too long.
And there was no hygiene, practically none.
So this is just going back for a thought.
Some of them-- there was not far from the camp where I lived, was a prisoner of war from France-- I mean, French prisoners that were kept until the war was over.
So they made friends.
And some of the Frenchmen promised the girls to bring them back to France to marry them.
Whether it was true, or they just wanted to romantically be together, I don't know.
But my plan was, with the majority of the girls, to contact-- the Red Cross was there right away-- to go back to Poland, go back definitely where they took me away from, to see [?
Bedzin. ?] I still didn't think that everybody is gone.
I wanted to see it.
And a lot of my friends, even now, said they never went back.
They were scared.
But this was my plan.
And I'm glad I did, because I did find some cousins-- not the older generation, but also people my age group that survived.
But it wasn't immediately.
So we stayed in the barracks, but not in the ones we took.
That's what the Red Cross suggested, and all the people in charge then.
There was another organization that was in charge, which was very important.
I have to mention it to you.
The Russian soldiers, the liberators, were drunk.
I, I'm not blaming them.
Now, I know they went through a lot.
I know they hated the Nazis.
But they were heavy drinkers.
And being blind drunk, they didn't care whether it was a German girl or a Jewish survivor, as long as she's a female.
And that was frightening.
That was really frightening.
You're talking about teenagers.
We were so scared of those drunk soldiers for rape, for, for, for coming in, for doing something.
After liberating us, we were afraid.
They were, they were really uncontrollable.
They were absolutely wild soldiers.
So there was an organization that was in the, in the forests.
They were partisans.
They were Czechoslovakian boys against the Nazis that were working in the underground organizations.
I remember they were wearing like red bands here.
And they came out with those short guns-- I don't know what you call the-- you know, like a short rifle, a little rifle.
And they were watching the camp.
They were surrounded.
And for a few days-- I'll never forget it-- we never got undressed, as little clothes as we had, whatever we wore, we were all in one place-- excuse me-- in one big room.
All the ladies, all the girls, stayed in one big room for a few nights.
We were afraid of the liberators.
And that was a terrible feeling, to be scared after being three years to, to work for such monsters.
Now, they explained to us, they were watching the camp.
They'll get-- finally, the Red Cross transported us, which took awhile.
But we were given food, and it wasn't like going to work.
It was-- I don't remember the voyage exactly, but I ended up coming back to my hometown.
I was back in Bedzin.
Since Bedzin is a smaller town, and it didn't have such a big organization, I ended up looking for my relatives in an adjacent town called Sosnowiec, which was a few kilometers away.
And it was a larger city.
In that town, everybody that survived would gather in one building, which was more or less-- I think the Polish government assigned it as a Jewish committee.
And everywhere on the walls, and on the doors, on the frames of the windows, were names.
It says, my name is so-and-so, I survived.
And there was no phone number.
Nobody had phones.
So we used to gather every single day, or a few times a day, to see which more names.
Then I found out later on, Jack was telling m the same thing was going on in Lodz, in Krakow, in every city in Poland.
That's how people found each other.
They would go to this building and read the names and see who was there.
And on one of my visits, I found in the court, in the yard, this cousin, this son, the two sisters, my mother's sister, that are those two boys, the youngest son that lives here, now.
He lived in Louisville, Kentucky, for a number of years.
He was interviewed.
Yesterday, he was on television.
He, when he saw me, this is a picture.
People-- well, first of all, people didn't think it's cousins.
It's sister and brother, and they found each other.
We were so emotional, so out of-- we just-- that he didn't know I'm alive.
And I didn't know he's alive.
And when he saw me, I couldn't control.
We both cried so much that he was afraid to tell me that his brother is alive until I calmed down.
And I got you.
I'm not alone anymore.
You're here.
We're together, we're together.
It was devastating, because he knew, he knew more than I did.
He went with my parents.
He was later on.
He was the last one to leave the ghetto.
He was in the ghetto with his mother, with my mother, with them together.
He was taken to a camp much later.
I was in 1942, so it was an awful long time for me not to be with my family.
So he set me down on the way, he says, you know, Max survived Auschwitz.
He's here, too.
So from then on, we lived together.
I mean, it was Max and Henry, the two brothers, me.
And then I found a close friend of his, which is his wife now.
I found her, and she moved in.
So actually, what we did-- what I call moved in, I'll explain to you.
My uncle, his father, Henry's and Max' father, was a coal miner, the only Jewish coal miner.
Because you never heard of something like that.
The Jews didn't have profession like that.
He was a coal miner.
He was digging coal from under the ground.
And he worked with all these hats that coal miners-- and I was so proud.
I had an uncle, a coal miner.
And he, unfortunately, he was with his sons in camp.
He never made it.
He didn't survive.
That was a separate story.
His son told me that my uncle's--

So because he was a coal miner, because we went back to the town where those boys lived, the mayor of the town assigned a nice apartment to the kids, to those two boys, to Max and Henry Rosmarin, because their father was a citizen.
He worked as a coal miner.
So we got a nice apartment.
This apartment was taken away from some Germans, probably.
I'm sure, because I'm talking about a location where this was not Bedzin.
This was [INAUDIBLE], where predominantly, the people are, like, half German, half Poles.
This was, like-- I don't know if you ever heard of Volksdeutsche.
When the Germans came in, they said they were Germans.
Then some Czechoslovakians did that, too.
When in the liberation came, they said, I never spoke German.
I was not a German.
They said that, but I know they did.
You see, once the government changed, they were something else.
That's so.
What can you do?
That's the way the world is.
So this apartment, which was very comfortable, so the girls had their own room.
So we, I, found this close friend of theirs, which then later on was Henry's-- is Henry's wife.
And Max, he's gone now.
He had, after the war, he had very heavy car accident, unfortunately.
He survived Auschwitz and was killed-- it was a head on collision in Bedford, Indiana.
He's gone now.
But I don't want to change the story.
But his wife was also a friend of ours.
And all week, all the girls, we lived together.
And when-- and I began to look for family while living with those two cousins of mine, and the girls.
I began to find, search in my head, the address of my aunt, which was so wonderful.
And I kept on saying to myself, if she was in hiding, it only makes sense that the Belgian people did not give them, did not squeal, or, or tell on them.
They must be alive somewhere.
And sure enough, it took months.
Through the Red Cross, I sent a letter from Poland, in Polish, to this address that I memorized in my head.
And what was such a miracle-- and I'll make it brief, because you don't have that much time, now-- this letter was sitting somewhere in a building that a nice-- somebody up there was helping me.
A nice, Gentile, Belgian lady saw the Polish stamp with the eagle.
And she said to herself, Mr.
And Mrs.
Gold-- that's my aunt and uncle-- I know where they are, now.
They are not here in this building.
But this letter must be very important, because it comes from Poland.
I'm going to try and contact them and see that they get it.
My aunt told me later how she found out.
And miracle-- six months later, I get a letter from Belgium.
We are delighted to find out you made it, you're in Poland, and where I was.
And right away, she sent me something, my sweet aunt.
She sent me a comb and some soap, stuff that you could use immediately.
And she kept on saying, right away, try to get out of Poland, come to Belgium.
Well, it was easier said than done.
Legally, I couldn't even get papers.
I had no birth certificate.
It was a mess.
I'm talking about 1945, 1946.
And here, I wasn't even anxious, because I lived with those guys.
They were very good to me.
They were older brothers, and they were providing food, and they were doing things for me, and I was never alone.
This was another fear.
From parents into camp, from camp, I was never independent.
Who is going doing to do something?
And here, I'm 18 years old.
What is this?
But this aunt was really insisting.
And I realized, and even my cousins, they, in that time, they were already kind of dating those girls, they were-- so I felt a little bit like an odd person.
OK?
They didn't make me feel that way, but I felt if Esther is dating Max, and Janet is dating Henry, and the two brides, the two-- I'm a sister, I'm a relative.
I mean, it's time to move on.
So I got on a train into Germany with a group of women, group of girls.
We were traveling.
I don't even know, we didn't have money.
We didn't have papers.
I managed to get into Germany.
And in Germany, I joined a displaced person, a DP camp, which was run by the American committee, the UNRRA.
They called it U-N-R-R-A, United Nations, some refugee something.
It was called UNRRA.
We called it UNRRA.
It's U-N-R-R-A.
And I joined that camp, because like I said, I didn't know how to live alone or what to do, how to make a living.
What will I do, go work?
I didn't know where to go.
So I joined this camp, and I knew that they will provide food.
And this camp was mostly inclined to organize or to propaganda for Zionism, OK, so that we should end up going to Israel, which was very enthusiastic, very appreciated.
This group-- I still have pictures, here-- that wanted all the young people to start a new life in Palestine.
Don't stay in German, don't go to any European-- come to Palestine.
The Zionist organization was working on us how to learn Hebrew, to be in a collective camp-- a collective, like a kibbutz, and go to Israel.
In the meantime, while I was being programmed and organized and having fun with those kids and singing Israeli, Hebrew songs, my aunt was working on how to get me out of there.
And she realized that with legal visas and stuff like that, that will take years.
So simply, she paid a guy 10,000 francs.
I don't know what the [? amount then, ?] the end of 1946, or the middle of-- and she wrote to me into that-- I wrote to her.
Wherever I went, I had already contact.
So I wrote to her from Germany, from Bergen-Belsen, that I'm in this and this camp, and we are being organized to go to Israel.
She didn't like it.
She said she wanted me to be with her.
She want-- she felt that she survived, and she's my father's sister.
I'll be her child.
Well, she sent me illegal man, a smuggler, which took us across the border.
Well, I must tell you, it was mixed feelings at that point.
I am young.
I never in my life was in an organized group.
I was never enthusiastic about Zionists, or about my own country, about Palestine.
And there is a big propaganda, which was, it was, you know, ironically, because the ones that were trying to speak and talk about it, they didn't go.
But they sent-- these were illegal exodus people, you know.
These were all--

And my aunt was older, and she realized this-- you survived the camps, and you're going to risk, now, to go with illegal, you know, where the English were arresting and sending to prison, and people were in camps.
You know, the exodus in Cyprus, Cyprus?
It was settling there.
But in my mind, I had mixed feelings.
Here, I made such good friends.
And I had a crush on a boy.
And I was feeling very good about it.
I wanted to be with them.
And my aunt sent me this guy.
But I said, goodbye, and I had nothing.
I had no luggage.
I looked like a mess at that time.
My hair started to grow already.
I had already, like, a little shorter than now, because now I'm cutting them short, again.
I went with this guy.
And he took me-- all night we walked through the border, which was-- I found out later, it was the route that they took Jack and his group of friends, they all took this route.
From the last German stop, which was Aachen in Bavaria, it was the last stop into Belgium.
And all the way when we walked, he kept on saying, your name is so-and-so.
He had some ID cards, in case they arrest me and send me back to Germany.
What people did, I heard from a lot, they arrest them, send them back to Germany.
They start-- you know what, that reminds me of the illegal aliens in California.
We were going back and coming back, again.
But I wasn't arrested.
But one moment, it was strange.
The guy-- I said to him, who are you?
What languages?
He said, he told me he speaks-- he spoke Polish with me.
He said, he speaks French, he speaks-- I don't know.
He was a professional smuggler.
He took money from my aunt to bring me.
And he delivered me.
She gave him a drink, a vodka, or something.
And she paid him out, I think it was a lot of money, 10,000 francs.
And he brought me there.
But while we were walking, he said to me, which I'll never forget, he said, you're a brave little girl, aren't you?
We are just here alone, and you trust me, and you go with me.
I'll never forget those words.
An 18-year-old-- and he spoke in Polish to me.
And I looked at him.
And I said, now, what would you do with a piece of nothing?
I said to him that way.
And he looked at me.
I said, look at me.
For 10,000 francs, you can get the nicest, the most beautiful woman in Brussels.
So he clapped me on my shoulder, and said, oh, you're a smart one.
And I wasn't scared, because I realized if he is a businessman, what would he do?
I mean, 10,000 francs, he could go to any nightclub and enjoy.
But when he looked at me, and he talked like that, for a moment, I said, gee, I did have courage, all by myself to come to my aunt's house.
When I arrived to my aunt's house, she bought me clothes, and she took me to show me around.
And this I must tell you.
I'm almost finished.
I am impressed.
I'll never forget, as long as my eyes are open, Brussels, Belgium.
I hadn't seen real chocolate or oranges or real food.
I can't believe it.

When my uncle took me to a store, and he said to me, you know, that Belgium-- Belgium, in those days, that was about summer of 1946-- said, Belgium was the only country in Europe that didn't suffer as much, maybe because the king was a collaborator.
Later on, they arrested the king of Belgium.
His son took the throne.
But that was another story I had, because King Leopold was working with the Germans.
But Belgium wasn't damaged as much, and they had plenty of stuff in the shops.
And I had never seen-- and I said, this is real, you can touch it, you can eat it?
He said, yes.
Your aunt will buy the food, and it will come home.
And I tell you, this is a picture.
When you live in poverty, when you live in, under, under, Nazis, all these years, you come to a country, when you see such beautiful food, I never wanted to leave Belgium.
I really didn't.
But then there was the Korean War, and Jack was afraid they will take him to the army.
And we never had the right as citizens to stay in Belgium.
Anyway, at that point, I was with my aunt and uncle.
And through a mutual friend, I met Jack.
I met Jack, and he was already in Belgium months before me.
And I'm-- his friend that was traveling with me, he met-- he told me about Jack, because he was also traveling the same route.
It all came around that time.
And that guy said to me, if anything happens to me, you tell whoever I'm going to.
And if anything would happen to me, he would go to my aunt.
So that was, like, an arrangement.
Fortunately, we weren't arrested.
We managed to come to Brussels.
And what my uncle did, which was smart, he took me to the police, and he said, here, she's an orphan, she's a refugee, she has nobody.
Can she get a card, a temporary stay, which he made me legal, which they did.
But on the card, every six months, I had to go and sign that I'm with my family.
And it said on the card, must emigrate-- in French, [INAUDIBLE] emigre.
And the two of us didn't like it.
Then when I met Jack, I didn't date him too long, because he was alone.
He had nobody.
He used to come to my aunt's house.
And my aunt would feed him, and she liked him.
And she was, literally, proposing for me.
I was only 19 years old, and she said to Jack, to-- something like, do you-- you guys are serious about each other?
I don't know why she was rushing it.
But she literally-- I never, he never said that he wants to marry me.
I really mean it.
That's why he's always joking.
He always talks to my daughters, he says, he was forced into marriage, which isn't true.
It's just that it came out from her.
She was very curious.
So maybe she was afraid that something-- in those days, something will go on.
Maybe, she was afraid that we are too anxious.
But we did, really needed each other very much.
And I didn't think it was fair to be under her, because whenever I came out, I went out with Jack, she would say, don't come home late, and stuff like that.
Now, when you're on your own for so many years, you don't like to be restricted and told to--
So I used to say, Jack, come on-- we used to speak Polish.
I didn't speak French then-- come with me, and tell aunt that I'm OK, or something.
Because I was afraid to open the door and walk into-- where did you go, what did you-- you know, something to that extent.
It didn't take too long, a few months.
And she made a little Jewish wedding, a chuppah.
Why I say Jewish wedding?
Because we didn't have any papers.
So for three years, I was Mademoiselle Szwarcfeld, living with Jack.
I knew I was married, [INAUDIBLE], but legally, we didn't get the papers until we decided to go to Australia.
And to go to Australia was only because the Belgium country is little.
I'm not blaming them.
Their constitution is very strict.
They don't probably want too many foreigners.
And they don't allow, they don't give you citizenship as easy as any other country, after five years or so.
So they just want you to go.
You stay as long as you stay.
If I would have stayed a little longer or lived there-- because we still had friends that stayed-- eventually, they got legal papers, and work, and everything.
But it was time to go.
And so we had to decide between America and Australia, because of a lot of very close friends.
And I also received letters.
These cousins of mine that I met in Poland and stayed with, they ended up in America.
They lived in Louisville, Kentucky.
And when I contacted them from Belgium, they didn't encourage us.
They didn't say, don't come to America.
But they said, if you could do better-- they didn't, because unemployment, it wasn't easy.
And they lived off some pension that Jewish welfare gave them.
So I mean, they didn't do that great.
So they were honest about that.
They said, look, if you-- and we heard great news from Australia.
They pick people literally from the boat.
Literally, they just waited for newcomers to come to work.
And right away, we became-- well, not right away, we had to make citizenship.
But it was a beautiful start to go to Australia.
1950, because most of my family was already in Israel, which was another story.
When the state of Israel was declared, my aunt and uncle, they were Zionists for years, they were dreaming to die in Israel.
Unfortunately, after two or three years, they went back to Europe.
And then they ended up here, which I followed them.
That's how I ended up coming to America, again.
So after 15 years-- so we moved in 1950-- OK, two young people who are starting new life.
Two years later, my older daughter was born, Dinah.
And then it wasn't that easy because of economy and all kinds of problems.
And my Dinah wasn't that healthy in the beginning.
I didn't have such a great pregnancy, so I wasn't rushing into having another child.
But then years go by, and you forget.
You forget the scare, you forget the pain.
And so I tried to get pregnant again, which took eight years.
So Silvia is eight years younger than Dinah.
Both were born in Melbourne.
And while I was pregnant with her, my aunt and uncle moved from Israel back to Belgium, from Belgium to America.
Oh, actually, no, no, no.
She moved.
She didn't go back to Belgium.
She had a sister, which I never met.
I have cousins in Chicago, first cousins, American-born, because she had a sister.
My father had sisters in Chicago, so they brought them, they brought my aunt and uncle.
But Chicago was too hard for them to live.
It was cold.
If they would stay in Chicago, we would have never leave Australia.
But once they came to California, and they start to write gorgeous letters.
You know, the sun is shining, and it's beautiful, and California was tempting.
And most of the people ask, what made you leave Australia after 15 years?
Well, I tell you, in those days, in the '60s, there was a big, like, a propaganda, like people were influenced-- Hollywood, California.
I don't know.
I just, it was-- and I also wanted to be back with them, with my family.
See, because Jack has nobody.
Not one single cousin survived.
They were all in the ghetto, and they all perished.
And to me, fortunately, some were in labor camps, and some were overseas, see, because my father had a big family overseas in Belgium.
I have, here, pictures of my cousins from France, survived in hiding.
Four-- three sisters and two brothers survived in hiding.
They ran to the south of France and survived, my father's sister's kids.
So I knew that there is somebody.
So that's how we ended up coming here.
And so we are here since 1965.
But life in Australia, it was a predominantly Jewish community.
We were very happy.
Jack was very active in, active in Jewish culture.
He loved it.
He still does love very much Jewish culture.
And the girls went to Jewish schools, but not religious, very secular, very-- but tradition, you know.
And so, and so here we are.
I guess what can I add?
What else do you need?
Well, it will be next tape.
[LAUGHTER]
